title: Inserting Nodes in a Linked List: A Step by Step Guide
summary: This video explains how to insert new nodes at specific points within a linked list. We'll cover the different scenarios, like inserting before or after existing elements and adding to an empty list.

sections.heading: Understanding Linked List Basics
sections.body: Before diving into insertion techniques, it's crucial to grasp the fundamental concept of a linked list. Imagine each node as a box containing data and a pointer directing us to the next box in line.
key_points: A 'node' is a self-contained unit with data
Nodes are connected via pointers forming a chain

sections.heading: Inserting Before an Existing Node
sections.body: To insert before a known node, we start by creating the new one and adjust its pointer to link backward. Then, redirect our predecessor's pointer towards this freshly inserted candidate.
key_points: Create a 'new node' with given data

sections.heading: Inserting After an Existing Node and at the Head
sections.body: 'After insertion' is simple when you have a reference to where new content should be, but inserting 'at the head', or beginning, requires reassigning this first pointer in our linked list.
key_points: Directly link after an existing node is straightforward
Head of list changes by moving current start

key_facts: Inserting at the head: previous->next = newNode; newNode->prev=NULL, newNode->next=head
